title: Getting Better Results from Cursor AI with Simple Rules
author: Andi Ashari
text:
Getting Better Results from Cursor AI with Simple Rules
Hey everyone! I’ve been playing around with Cursor AI lately, and it’s been super helpful for coding. But sometimes, it needs a little guidance to work just right. I put together a simple set of rules to make it better at fixing bugs and building new stuff. I thought I’d share them with you in case you want to try them too! You can find them all in my gist here:
https://gist.github.com/aashari/07cc9c1b6c0debbeb4f4d94a3a81339e
What’s Inside?
I split everything into three files because I wanted to keep things simple and clear. Each one has a job to do:
core.md
This is the main file with the basic rules I want Cursor AI to follow all the time. It’s like the foundation for everything else. I stick this in my project’s .cursorrules file or add it to Cursor’s settings (you can find that by hitting Cmd + Shift + P, then going to Rules > User Rules in the sidebar). It makes sure the AI stays on track no matter what I’m doing.
request.md
This is for starting something new, like adding a feature or tweaking code. I use it to tell the AI exactly what I want — like “build this thing for me” — and it helps keep the work neat and organized from the start.
refresh.md
This one’s for when things go wrong — like when I see an error that won’t go away, or the AI keeps looking at the same files and missing other stuff that might matter. I use this to tell it, “Hey, look at everything again and fix this properly.” It’s saved me a bunch of time when debugging gets messy.
Why I Made These
I love how fast Cursor AI can write code, but sometimes it gets stuck or goes off in the wrong direction. Like, when I’m fixing a bug, it might keep tweaking one file and forget to check if something else is breaking it. Or when I ask for a new feature, I want it to fit nicely with the rest of my project — not just slap some random code in there. So, I wrote these rules to give it a clear plan to follow. It’s been working great for me, and I figured it might help someone else too.
How to Use Them
It’s pretty easy to get going with these. Here’s what I do:
- Grab the files from my gist: https://gist.github.com/aashari/07cc9c1b6c0debbeb4f4d94a3a81339e. You can download them or copy them straight out.
- Put core.md into your project’s .cursorrules file or paste it into Cursor’s User Rules section. This sets up the basic stuff the AI should always do.
- For fixing bugs, I take refresh.md, swap out the placeholder part (it says something like {my query}), and type in what’s wrong — like “the login button still crashes.” Then I send it to the AI.
- For new stuff, I use request.md the same way. I replace {my request} with whatever I need, like “add a save button,” and let the AI take it from there.
That’s it! It’s not fancy, but it gets the job done.
What I’ve Learned
Using these rules has made my coding smoother. The AI doesn’t just throw quick fixes at me anymore — it actually thinks about how everything fits together. For example, when I used refresh.md to fix a bug, it stopped looping on the same file and checked other places that were causing trouble. And with request.md, my new features feel like they belong in the project, not like some random add-on.
One tip: if you try these, make sure to fill in the placeholders with details. The more you tell the AI, the better it works. Like, don’t just say “fix it” — say “fix the error on line 20 in user.js.” It makes a big difference.
Try It Out!
If you’re using Cursor AI — or even something similar — these rules might save you some headaches. They’re not perfect, but they’ve been working for me, and I’m still tweaking them as I go. Check out the gist here: https://gist.github.com/aashari/07cc9c1b6c0debbeb4f4d94a3a81339e, and let me know what you think! Got ideas to make them better? I’d love to hear them.
Happy coding!
